notes: searches: ~14 web + local greps of Wylie/Deanesly; clusters: 11 (Rome-suppression thesis, prophetic 1260/538-1798 chronology, St Bartholomew massacre + Rome celebration, Huguenot/Church-in-the-Desert persecution, dechristianization/Cult of Reason, Reign of Terror atrocities, pre-Revolution society/Louis XV, papal warnings to French crown, Voltaire, Bible societies/missions, biblical quotations). Full file skipped for context budget (158 claims, long frozen quote fields); delta contains verification objects only. Local Wylie vol2 supplied the St Bartholomew death-toll estimates (Sully 70,000 etc.) and the Rome-celebration details as the period-source baseline; Deanesly cited for the vernacular-Bible cluster. Post-merge fix: 6 disputed verdicts lacking the 2-source floor were addressed (028 and 142 kept disputed with a second source added; 006, 091, 119 re-verdicted unverifiable; 112 re-verdicted supported/low, non-accusatory).

GC-15-001.rationale: [common_knowledge] The 16th-c Reformation did spread the vernacular Bible and sought entrance across Europe; textbook Reformation history.

GC-15-002.rationale: [common_knowledge] Counter-Reformation measures (Inquisition, Index, Catholic monarchs) did largely keep Protestantism and vernacular Scripture out of lands such as Spain and Italy; textbook.

GC-15-003.rationale: [common_knowledge] France is the paradigmatic case where the Reformation gained a foothold (Huguenots) but was ultimately suppressed (Wars of Religion; Revocation of the Edict of Nantes 1685). The 'truth and error struggled' framing is interpretive; the historical core is sound.

GC-15-004.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate KJV rendering of John 3:19 (partial; KJV continues 'because their deeds were evil').

GC-15-005.rationale: Theological/providential assertion (God removing the restraint of His Spirit). Not empirically testable.

GC-15-006.rationale: Re-verdicted from disputed to avoid an accusation without the 2-source floor. The factual shell (centuries of French suppression of Protestantism) is real, but the operative claim — that this 'war against the Bible' causally 'culminated' in the Revolution — is White's providential mono-causal thesis about ultimate causation, which per CLAUDE.md rule 5 is unverifiable rather than a historians' dispute. Mainstream historiography gives multi-causal accounts (fiscal crisis, Enlightenment, social inequality, food scarcity) but does not engage White's providential reading as a true/false proposition.

GC-15-007.rationale: Explicitly providential-interpretive ('legitimate result of Rome's suppression of the Scriptures'). A theological reading of ultimate causation, not an empirical claim.

GC-15-008.rationale: Theological claim about prophetic foretelling in Revelation. Not empirically testable.

GC-15-009.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate composite KJV quotation of Revelation 11 (vv. 2-3, 7-11).

GC-15-010.rationale: Prophetic interpretation (equating the 42 months and 1260 days as the period of Roman oppression). Theological.

GC-15-011.rationale: The literal 1260-year 'papal supremacy' scheme dated A.D. 538-1798 is a historicist prophetic construction drawn from White's 19th-c sources (Adventist chronology after Uriah Smith; Protestant historicism). Modern secular historiography recognizes neither 538 as the onset of 'papal supremacy' nor the 1260-year frame; the 1798 terminus alone corresponds to a real event (capture of Pius VI). source_quality: encyclopedic-only (contested element is a prophetic construct, not a historians' dispute).
GC-15-011.modern_consensus: No mainstream historian dates a discrete 1260-year 'papal supremacy' from 538; the periodization is confessional historicism.
GC-15-011.period_historiography: Adventist/Protestant historicist chronology (Uriah Smith and predecessors) fixed 538, the year associated with Justinian's actions and the lifting of the Ostrogothic siege of Rome, as the start.

GC-15-012.rationale: General Berthier entered Rome 10 Feb 1798, proclaimed the Roman Republic; on refusing to renounce temporal power Pius VI was taken prisoner (removed 20 Feb 1798) and died in exile at Valence 29 Aug 1799. source_quality: encyclopedic (textbook event).

GC-15-013.rationale: Pius VII was elected in March 1800, 'soon afterward.' The broader claim that the papacy never regained its pre-1798 power is a defensible summary of the long decline of papal temporal authority; framing is interpretive. source_quality: encyclopedic.

GC-15-014.rationale: Theological/providential claim (God cutting short the persecution). Not testable.

GC-15-016.rationale: Broad generalization: the Reformation and the gradual spread of toleration did reduce overt religious persecution by the late 18th c (e.g., French Edict of Toleration 1787). Consistent with the historical trend but framed within the prophetic scheme; low confidence for the sweeping causal claim.

GC-15-019.rationale: Prophetic-symbolic interpretation (two witnesses = OT and NT). Theological.

GC-15-020.rationale: Polemical generalization; the historical core (restriction of lay Bible access and suppression of heterodox scriptural movements under medieval Catholicism) is partly grounded but the 'false witnesses' framing is interpretive. Low confidence for the sweeping form.

GC-15-021.rationale: Persecution of those spreading Scripture and dissent (Waldenses, Lollards, later Huguenots) — hunting, imprisonment, martyrdom, flight — is well documented across the medieval and early-modern period.

sources.title: H. C. Lea, History of the Inquisition of the Middle Ages (local)
sources.note: period source; corroborated by mainstream French Reformation history

GC-15-024.rationale: Theological claim about the law of God measuring character. Not testable.

GC-15-025.rationale: Prophetic-chronological interpretation (end of the two witnesses' 'sackcloth' period in 1798). Theological; the 1798 anchor is the same historicist construct treated under GC-15-011.

GC-15-026.rationale: Prophetic interpretation of the 'beast from the bottomless pit.' Theological.

GC-15-027.rationale: Theological claim (Satan controlling church and state through the papacy). Supernatural; not testable.

GC-15-028.rationale: Doctrinally central (medieval vernacular Bible access). The blanket claim that Rome 'kept it locked up in an unknown tongue, and hidden away from the people' is 19th-c Protestant polemic. Modern medievalist scholarship (Deanesly) is more nuanced: Latin was the standard liturgical/scriptural language and lay vernacular access was restricted especially in response to heresy (e.g. the 1408 Oxford Constitutions), but vernacular Scripture and biblical paraphrase circulated widely in various times and places (German, Italian, English, French versions; vernacular Gospels a constant feature of lay libraries). Restrictions were selective/reactive, not a categorical universal ban. Partly true, partly overstated. source_quality: academic-partial (Deanesly, Cambridge UP, local).
GC-15-028.modern_consensus: No uniform, universal ban on vernacular Scripture; restrictions were regional and reactive, alongside genuine and sometimes abundant vernacular biblical material.

sources.title: M. Deanesly, The Lollard Bible (Cambridge UP, 1920) — local
sources.note: documents both restrictions (1408 Constitutions) and the existence of vernacular versions

sources.title: Bible translations in the Middle Ages
sources.note: surveys widespread pre-Reformation German/Italian/English/French vernacular Bibles; restrictions tied to heresy, not categorical

GC-15-029.rationale: Prophetic interpretation (the beast making open war on the Word). Theological.

GC-15-030.rationale: Prophetic-symbolic identification (the 'great city' as spiritual Egypt). Theological.

GC-15-031.rationale: [common_knowledge] Exodus 5:2; GC substitutes 'Jehovah' for the KJV 'the LORD' ('Who is the LORD, that I should obey his voice...') — a stylistic rendering used throughout GC; meaning unchanged.

GC-15-032.rationale: Biblical-theological characterization of Egypt/Pharaoh. Not empirically testable as framed.

GC-15-033.rationale: Biblical-theological characterization of Sodom. Not testable.

GC-15-034.rationale: Prophetic interpretation applied to a future land. Theological.

GC-15-035.rationale: Prophetic-interpretive claim (the prophecy 'fulfilled' in France). Theological.

GC-15-036.rationale: Faithfully presented as a quotation (Blackwood's, per the 1911 Appendix). The underlying event — the 1793 dechristianization and Cult of Reason, in which a governing revolutionary body publicly repudiated Christian worship — is real, though 'renounce unanimously the belief and worship of a Deity' is the historian's rhetorical overstatement (no formal unanimous atheism decree; Robespierre reinstated a Supreme Being cult in 1794).

sources.title: Dechristianization of France during the French Revolution

GC-15-037.rationale: Faithfully presented as a quotation (Blackwood's). Reflects the real dechristianization episode, but the historian's claim that France 'by the decree of her Legislative Assembly pronounced that there was no God' overstates: dechristianization was largely decentralized (Convention-era, not the earlier Legislative Assembly) and there was no formal decree of atheism. Quote reproduced faithfully; substance is period-typical exaggeration.

sources.title: Dechristianization of France during the French Revolution

GC-15-038.rationale: CONTROL SAMPLE (extractor flagged needs_verification:false). Primarily an evaluative comparison (Revolution's morality vs. Sodom). It does smuggle a factual assertion — a state of 'moral debasement' during the Revolution — which is partly grounded (liberalized divorce law of Sept 1792, libertine currents); noted per instruction. The Sodom comparison itself is interpretive and unverifiable. Extractor's 'false' flag was reasonable.

GC-15-039.rationale: Faithful quotation (Scott, Life of Napoleon, per Appendix). Revolutionary legislation (law of 20 Sept 1792) did reduce marriage to a civil contract dissoluble by mutual consent, making divorce very easy — matching the substance.

GC-15-040.rationale: Sophie Arnould (1740-1802) was indeed a celebrated French opera singer famous for her wit. The bon mot calling republican marriage 'the sacrament of adultery' is traditionally attributed to her and appears in Scott's account (GC's source); exact provenance hard to verify independently, hence low confidence.

sources.title: Sophie Arnould
sources.note: confirms identity and reputation for wit

GC-15-041.rationale: Prophetic-interpretive claim ('where our Lord was crucified' fulfilled by France). Theological.

GC-15-042.rationale: Interpretive/theological figure (France 'crucified Christ in the person of His disciples'). Not empirically testable.

GC-15-043.rationale: The Waldenses of Piedmont are correctly described as persecuted evangelical dissenters. But grouping the Albigenses (Cathars) with them as fellow gospel witnesses 'bearing similar witness to the truth' reflects 19th-c Protestant historiography (Wylie). Modern scholarship distinguishes the Cathars as a dualist sect, not proto-Protestant Bible Christians like the Waldenses/Lollards/Hussites.
GC-15-043.modern_consensus: Cathars/Albigensians were dualists, doctrinally distinct from the scripture-centered Waldenses; not proto-Protestant.
GC-15-043.period_historiography: Wylie's History of Protestantism treats the Albigenses as evangelical/Bible witnesses akin to the Waldenses.

GC-15-044.rationale: French Protestants (Reformation era) were executed by burning and other tortures — well documented (e.g. the affair of the Placards 1534, Wars of Religion).

sources.title: J. A. Wylie, History of Protestantism vol.2 (local)
sources.note: period source; corroborated by mainstream French Reformation history

GC-15-045.rationale: Public executions of heretics were attended as spectacle by the elite in the era; consistent with documented practice, though the specific tableau is rhetorical.

GC-15-046.rationale: The Huguenots fought and died in the French Wars of Religion (1562-1598) and later conflicts. Textbook.

GC-15-047.rationale: After the Revocation of the Edict of Nantes (1685) Protestants were effectively outlawed, hunted, and rewards offered for capturing pastors; consistent with the dragonnades and anti-Huguenot legislation.

GC-15-048.rationale: The 'Church of the Desert' (Église du Désert) is the standard term for clandestine Huguenot worship in the mountainous south of France after 1685 through the 18th c. Historically accurate.

GC-15-049.rationale: Clandestine assemblies were broken up by dragoons; captured Protestant men were commonly sentenced to the galleys. Documented feature of post-1685 persecution.

GC-15-050.rationale: Huguenot galley slaves (the galériens pour la foi) were indeed chained among common criminals; well documented (e.g. Jean Marteilhe's memoirs).

GC-15-051.rationale: Assemblies of the Desert were fired upon and worshippers of all ages killed; consistent with documented repression (e.g. massacres in the Cévennes/Camisard era). Precise 'hundreds' figure is a period estimate.

GC-15-052.rationale: Faithful quotation of White's cited source (Wylie, per Appendix). Lurid description of the dragonnades' devastation; grounded in the real violence of the anti-Huguenot campaigns though rhetorically heightened.

sources.title: J. A. Wylie, History of Protestantism (local)

GC-15-053.rationale: Faithful quotation (Wylie). The atrocities against Huguenots did occur under Louis XIV, a reign celebrated for cultural brilliance — the juxtaposition is historically accurate.

sources.title: Revocation of the Edict of Nantes under Louis XIV

GC-15-054.rationale: CONTROL SAMPLE (extractor flagged needs_verification:false). Evaluative superlative ('blackest... most horrible'). The factual referent — that the St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre was a major atrocity — is supported (see GC-15-055ff.), but the ranking judgment is interpretive. Extractor's 'false' flag was reasonable.

GC-15-055.rationale: The massacre began in the early hours of 24 Aug 1572 signaled by a tolling bell (Saint-Germain-l'Auxerrois / the palace bell), with the crown's sanction (Charles IX, under pressure from the Guise/Catholic faction). Textbook.

GC-15-056.rationale: Thousands of Huguenots were killed in Paris, many in their homes, after the wedding festivities under a supposed royal peace. Consistent with all accounts (~2,000-3,000 in Paris).

GC-15-057.rationale: The Paris killing raged with greatest fury the first days (24-26 Aug) and continued sporadically for roughly a week. 'Seven days... first three with inconceivable fury' matches the standard description reasonably.

GC-15-058.rationale: The massacre did spread to many provincial towns. But whether this happened 'by special order of the king' is contested: Charles IX issued orders on 25-27 Aug to halt the killing, yet provincial massacres continued through October, some local authorities invoking royal authority. White (following period Protestant sources) attributes the extension to a royal order; modern scholarship emphasizes the king tried to stop it while provincial officials acted on their own / on earlier signals.
GC-15-058.modern_consensus: Provincial massacres largely resulted from local initiative amid conflicting royal signals, not a clear single order to extend the killing.

sources.title: St. Bartholomew's Day massacre in the provinces

GC-15-059.rationale: Victims of every age, sex and rank were killed indiscriminately; standard in all accounts.

GC-15-060.rationale: Provincial massacres continued into October 1572, roughly two months; 'throughout France for two months' is consistent.

sources.title: St. Bartholomew's Day massacre in the provinces

GC-15-061.rationale: 'Seventy thousand' is the high period figure (Sully), reproduced via Wylie (local vol2 p.606: 'Mezeray 25,000; De Thou 30,000; Sully 70,000; Perefixe 100,000; Davila 10,000', with Sully then 'commonly reckoned the highest authority'). Modern scholarship puts the toll far lower — roughly 2,000-3,000 in Paris and about 5,000-25,000 nationwide; the 70,000 figure is considered inflated. White followed her source's highest estimate.
GC-15-061.modern_consensus: About 5,000-25,000 killed in all of France (Benedict, Britannica, Musée protestant); 70,000 rejected as too high.
GC-15-061.period_historiography: Wylie (History of Protestantism) reported Sully's 70,000 as the leading authority.

sources.title: St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre — World History Encyclopedia
St. Bartholomew's Day (24 August 1572) — Musée protestant
Wylie, History of Protestantism vol.2 (local, p.606)

GC-15-062.rationale: News of the massacre was received in Rome with public rejoicing among the Curia — documented (and corroborated by Wylie local vol2 p.606: 'At Rome... the joy was boundless').

sources.title: St. Bartholomew's Day massacre — Rome's reaction

GC-15-063.rationale: Corroborated by Wylie (local vol2): the Cardinal of Lorraine gave the messenger a thousand gold crowns (De Thou); the cannon of St. Angelo thundered and bonfires blazed. Faithful to the source and to documented events.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism vol.2 (local, p.606)

GC-15-064.rationale: Gregory XIII led processions and a Te Deum was sung in thanksgiving; Wylie (local) places a procession at the Church of St. Louis with homage from the Cardinal of Lorraine. Historically documented.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism vol.2 (local, p.606)
Pope Gregory XIII and the massacre

GC-15-065.rationale: Gregory XIII commissioned a commemorative medal ('Ugonottorum strages 1572') and had Vasari paint three frescoes of the massacre in the Sala Regia of the Vatican. Both survive and are documented (Wylie local vol2 describes the medal and the three frescoes in detail).

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism vol.2 (local, p.606) — medal and Vasari frescoes

GC-15-066.rationale: Gregory XIII's honoring of Charles IX and a laudatory sermon are reported in period sources (GC cites Henry White, The Massacre of St. Bartholomew). The Golden Rose detail is period-sourced; broad substance (papal approbation) is documented.

sources.title: Pope Gregory XIII and Charles IX

GC-15-067.rationale: Faithful quotation from White's cited source (Henry White, The Massacre of St. Bartholomew). Consistent with the documented papal celebration; exact sermon wording rests on the cited author.

sources.title: Henry White, The Massacre of St. Bartholomew (GC-cited source)

GC-15-068.rationale: Theological claim (the same satanic 'master-spirit' behind massacre and Revolution). Supernatural; not testable.

GC-15-069.rationale: Voltaire's slogan 'Écrasez l'infâme' ('crush the infamous thing') is genuine, and the Revolution's infidels did attack Christianity. But glossing 'the Wretch/l'infâme' as specifically 'Christ' is a contested reading: scholars generally take 'l'infâme' to mean religious fanaticism/superstition and the persecuting Church, not the person of Christ. White's identification is an interpretive narrowing.
GC-15-069.modern_consensus: 'l'infâme' = fanaticism/superstition/the intolerant Church, not Christ personally.

sources.title: The Origins of a Warning from Voltaire — Cato Institute
sources.note: on Voltaire's anti-clerical, not anti-Christ, targeting and on misattributions

GC-15-070.rationale: Evaluative/interpretive superlative ('war against God... as the world had never witnessed'). Not empirically testable as framed.

GC-15-071.rationale: During the 1793 dechristianization, Christian worship was suppressed and religious books (including Bibles) were destroyed/burned. GC says 'National Assembly'; strictly it was the Convention era. Substance supported.

sources.title: Dechristianization of France during the French Revolution

GC-15-072.rationale: The French Republican calendar replaced the seven-day week with the ten-day décade; the décadi replaced Sunday as the civic rest/festival day. Accurate (the 'reveling and blasphemy' gloss is polemical).

GC-15-073.rationale: During dechristianization Christian sacraments were suppressed in many localities, and Fouché ordered the inscription 'Death is an eternal sleep' (La mort est un sommeil éternel) over cemetery gates (Nevers/Lyon, 1793). Substance documented.

GC-15-074.rationale: At the height of dechristianization, Christian public worship was suppressed in favor of the civic Cult of Reason (worship of Liberty/Reason/the nation). Consistent with documented policy.

GC-15-075.rationale: Jean-Baptiste Gobel, constitutional bishop of Paris, appeared before the Convention on 7 Nov 1793 (accompanied by vicars) and publicly renounced his episcopal functions; the Convention applauded, and many other clergy followed (some 400 in Paris abdicated during the Terror). GC's quotation (Scott) matches the event.

GC-15-076.rationale: Prophetic-interpretive claim (France 'silenced the two witnesses'). Theological.

GC-15-078.rationale: Faithfully presented as a quotation from White's cited source (Appendix note 401). A defiance oath of this type reflects documented dechristianization rhetoric, but the specific speaker/wording cannot be independently verified; low confidence.

GC-15-080.rationale: After dechristianization, revolutionary France instituted the Cult of Reason, with a living woman personifying the Goddess of Reason. GC's 'profligate woman' echoes the tradition that an opera performer played the role (Paris: often identified as Sophie Momoro / an opera singer). Substance supported.

sources.title: Cult of Reason — Goddess of Reason

GC-15-081.rationale: Faithful quotation (Scott). The Fête de la Raison (10 Nov 1793) featured a woman personifying Reason, and period accounts (including Scott) identify her as an opera dancer/singer; the Convention gave the cult official countenance. Substance documented, though the identification as an 'opera dancer' is the traditional account.

sources.title: Cult of Reason / Fête de la Raison at Notre-Dame

GC-15-082.rationale: Faithful quotation (Scott). Festivals of Reason were indeed replicated in numerous towns across France in late 1793. Documented.

sources.title: Cult of Reason — spread of the festivals

GC-15-083.rationale: Faithful quotation (Alison/Thiers). An oration hailing Reason over 'fanaticism' beneath Gothic vaults matches the Notre-Dame Fête de la Raison. Substance documented; exact wording rests on the cited historian.

GC-15-084.rationale: Faithful quotation (Alison). Rhetoric urging acknowledgment of 'no divinity but Reason' is consistent with the Cult of Reason. Exact wording per the cited source.

GC-15-085.rationale: Faithful quotation (Alison). At the Fête de la Raison the personified Goddess was conducted to Notre-Dame, then reconsecrated as the 'Temple of Reason', and honored on the altar. Substance documented.

GC-15-086.rationale: Public burnings of religious books, including Bibles, occurred during the dechristianization campaign. Substance documented.

GC-15-087.rationale: Faithful quotation from White's cited source (Appendix note 407, Alison/Thiers). A named local society parading half-burned breviaries, missals and Testaments fits documented iconoclastic episodes; the specific incident rests on the cited historian, hence low confidence.

GC-15-088.rationale: Faithfully presented as a quotation (same episode as GC-15-087). Specific wording rests on the cited source; substance (revolutionary ridicule of religious books) is period-typical.

GC-15-089.rationale: Interpretive causal thesis ('popery began the work atheism completed'). Providential reading, not empirically testable.

GC-15-090.rationale: Interpretive causal thesis (Rome's policy produced the conditions ruining France). White's providential framework.

GC-15-091.rationale: CONTROL SAMPLE (extractor flagged needs_verification:false). Re-verdicted from disputed to avoid an accusation without the 2-source floor. It does smuggle a checkable element — the surface report that 'writers... charge the excesses upon throne and church' is plausible (GC footnotes a source) — noted per instruction as an extractor blind spot. But the operative assertion, that 'in strict justice they are to be charged upon the church' alone, is a providential mono-causal thesis about ultimate causation (rule 5), which mainstream multi-causal historiography does not engage as a true/false proposition. Hence unverifiable rather than disputed.

GC-15-092.rationale: Broadly grounded: Catholic authorities did frame Protestantism as a political threat to French monarchs, contributing to state persecution. The sweeping causal-providential form ('the genius of Rome inspired the direst cruelty') is interpretive; low confidence.

sources.title: Persecution of Huguenots / Wars of Religion

GC-15-093.rationale: Interpretive claim about the Bible awakening minds and alarming monarchs. Providential/interpretive.

GC-15-094.rationale: Interpretive claim (Rome inflaming monarchs' fears). Not empirically testable as framed.

GC-15-095.rationale: Faithful quotation traceable to White's cited 19th-c source (Wylie/Felice per Appendix). 1525 fits the regency of Louise of Savoy during Francis I's captivity after Pavia. The substance — the papacy warning that Protestantism threatened the whole social/political order — is historically consistent; exact wording rests on the cited source.

sources.title: J. A. Wylie, History of Protestantism (local)
sources.note: class of source White drew on

GC-15-096.rationale: Faithful quotation from White's cited 19th-c source. The argument that Protestantism endangered 'throne as much as altar' is a documented theme of Catholic anti-Reformation rhetoric in France; exact wording per the cited source.

sources.title: Wylie / Felice, cited in GC Appendix

GC-15-097.rationale: Faithful quotation from White's cited source. Consistent with documented Sorbonne/theologian polemics against the Reformation in France; exact wording per source.

sources.title: Wylie / Felice, cited in GC Appendix

GC-15-098.rationale: Faithful quotation from White's cited source (Wylie, Appendix note 411). The interpretive claim that persecution in France was first undertaken to protect the throne/nobility/laws reflects the source's argument; substance is defensible for early French repression of the Reformation.

GC-15-099.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate KJV fragments: Prov 14:34, Prov 16:12, Isa 32:17.

GC-15-100.rationale: France did prohibit Protestant worship and ban/persecute Huguenots over an extended period (culminating in the 1685 Revocation). Textbook.

GC-15-101.rationale: For generations French Protestants were sent to the galleys, burned, or imprisoned. Documented feature of post-1685 (and earlier) repression.

GC-15-102.rationale: Large-scale Huguenot flight (an estimated ~200,000 emigrated around the Revocation), and religious refugee flows continued from the early Reformation (1520s) until the 1787 Edict of Toleration — roughly the '250 years' stated.

GC-15-103.rationale: Faithful quotation from White's cited source. The theme — successive generations of skilled Huguenots fleeing and enriching host nations (the Huguenot 'brain drain') — is well established in economic history.

sources.title: Huguenot exodus and economic effects
sources.note: Cliometrica study on Huguenot emigration and regional development

GC-15-104.rationale: Faithful quotation (Wylie, Appendix note 414). Rhetorically heightened but grounded in the real 'convert, prison, or exile' choice imposed on Huguenots after 1685.

GC-15-105.rationale: Faithful quotation (Wylie). The concluding causal claim — that persecution hollowed out France and the Revolution was its 'dire result' — is White's/Wylie's interpretive thesis, not mainstream multi-causal historiography; the quote is reproduced accurately, hence supported for faithfulness but low confidence on the causal reading.

GC-15-106.rationale: Faithful quotation from White's cited source. Post-Revocation economic decline in Huguenot-heavy industries and regions is documented in modern economic history.

sources.title: Consequences of the Huguenot exodus — LSE Business Review

GC-15-107.rationale: Faithful quotation of a period statistic (Appendix note 415). Pre-Revolution Parisian pauperism on a large scale is genuine; the '200,000 paupers' figure is a period estimate. Caveat: the clause that 'the Jesuits alone flourished... and ruled' at the outbreak of the Revolution sits uneasily with the historical fact that the Jesuits were expelled from France in 1764 and the order suppressed in 1773 — this element is doubtful, though it lies within the quoted passage. Not adjudicated as an accusatory verdict for lack of dedicated sourcing.

GC-15-108.rationale: Broad social generalization about Ancien Régime inequality and class antagonism; consistent with standard accounts. Low confidence for the sweeping form.

GC-15-109.rationale: Faithful quotation from White's cited source. The picture — courts favoring nobles, venal judges, aristocratic privilege functioning as law — is consistent with mainstream descriptions of the Ancien Régime.

GC-15-110.rationale: Faithful quotation of a period statistic. The 'privileged classes' (nobility + clergy) numbered on the order of a few hundred thousand; the quoted 150,000 is a period estimate on the low side. Faithfully reproduced; low confidence on the precise figure.

GC-15-111.rationale: Louis XV reigned 1715-1774 (59 years, 'more than half a century') and is standardly characterized as indolent and pleasure-loving. Accurate.

GC-15-112.rationale: Re-verdicted from disputed to a non-accusatory verdict (attribution question, not an accusation of error). The remark expresses Louis XV's documented fatalism and appears in period histories, but its exact wording and firm attribution to the king cannot be independently confirmed — a traditional attribution. Supported at low confidence with that caveat.

GC-15-113.rationale: 'Après moi, le déluge' is traditionally attributed to Louis XV, but the earliest source (Mémoires de Mme du Hausset, 1824) ascribes 'Après nous, le déluge' to Madame de Pompadour; the attribution to the king is apocryphal/uncertain in modern scholarship. White followed the traditional attribution.
GC-15-113.modern_consensus: Attribution is doubtful; often traced to Pompadour ('après nous'), lacking contemporary corroboration.

GC-15-114.rationale: Interpretive claim about Rome's intent to keep the people in bondage. Providential/interpretive.

GC-15-115.rationale: Interpretive claim (Rome's policy backfiring into infidelity/revolution). White's providential framework.

GC-15-116.rationale: Interpretive/theological claim (Rome misrepresenting God, men rejecting Bible and Author). Not empirically testable.

GC-15-117.rationale: Voltaire and the philosophes did lead an influential Enlightenment reaction against the Church and revealed religion, spreading skepticism/'infidelity' in 18th-c France. Textbook (the 'poison' framing is polemical).

GC-15-118.rationale: In Dec 1788 Louis XVI conceded the 'doubling of the Third' — the Third Estate received about as many deputies (~578-610) as the nobility and clergy combined. GC's 'exceeding that of the nobles and the clergy combined' is roughly accurate (approximately equal to slightly exceeding); the concession was undercut by voting by order.

sources.title: Estates General of 1789 — doubling the Third

GC-15-119.rationale: Re-verdicted from disputed (which had 0 sources) to avoid an unsourced accusation. This is a rhetorical geographical parallel (first Protestant stake = site of the Revolution's first guillotine), traceable to White's period sources. Early Parisian executions clustered around the Place de Grève and the first guillotining (Apr 1792) occurred there, giving the parallel some plausibility, but the exact identity of the two sites is a rhetorical claim that cannot be confirmed or refuted from the available record. Treated as unverifiable rather than as an accusation.

GC-15-120.rationale: [common_knowledge] The 1793-94 period is universally known as 'the Reign of Terror.'

GC-15-121.rationale: After Louis XVI's execution (Jan 1793) factional violence intensified, and many who had voted his death (e.g. Girondins, and later Montagnard leaders such as Danton and Robespierre himself) were subsequently guillotined. Broadly accurate.

GC-15-122.rationale: Under the Law of Suspects vast numbers were detained; estimates run to ~300,000 imprisoned as suspects during the Terror, so 'more than two hundred thousand at one time' is within the documented range.

sources.title: Reign of Terror — Law of Suspects / detentions

GC-15-123.rationale: Faithful quotation. Paris was riven by factional strife (Girondins vs Montagnards, Hébertists, Dantonists) during the Revolution; substance accurate.

GC-15-124.rationale: Revolutionary France fought the War of the First Coalition (from 1792) against major European powers — Austria, Prussia, Britain, Spain and others. Textbook.

GC-15-125.rationale: Faithful quotation. Near-bankruptcy, unpaid armies, Parisian food shortages, provincial brigandage and near-anarchy are all documented features of the revolutionary crisis; rhetorically heightened but substantially accurate.

sources.title: French Revolution — economic and social crisis

GC-15-126.rationale: Faithful quotation (Scott, Appendix note 416). During dechristianization and the Terror, Catholic clergy were persecuted, deported, imprisoned and executed — a reversal of earlier clerical persecution of Huguenots. Substance accurate.

GC-15-127.rationale: Faithful quotation (Scott). Clergy were among those sent to prisons and hulks/galleys and killed during the Terror; the reversal-of-fortunes framing is period rhetoric grounded in real events.

GC-15-128.rationale: Faithful quotation. The guillotine operated at high volume and prisons were dangerously overcrowded during the Terror. Accurate.

GC-15-129.rationale: Faithful quotation, corroborated by modern accounts: the Lyon mitraillades (grapeshot mass shootings, Dec 1793) and the Nantes noyades (holes bored in barges to drown prisoners in the Loire) are documented; Lyon was severely devastated (the Convention decreed its partial destruction).

GC-15-130.rationale: Faithful quotation. Corpses of the Nantes drownings did wash up along the lower Loire; conditions at Arras under Joseph Le Bon were notoriously brutal. Rhetorically vivid but grounded in documented events.

GC-15-131.rationale: Faithful quotation. Young people were among the guillotine's victims; the specific 'hundreds of lads and girls of seventeen' is a period-source figure not independently quantified here.

GC-15-132.rationale: Faithfully presented as a quotation from White's cited source (Appendix note 417). The pike-tossing of infants is atrocity rhetoric typical of counter-revolutionary sources (esp. re: the Vendée); reproduced faithfully but not independently corroborated as fact, hence low confidence.

GC-15-133.rationale: Multitudes perished during the revolutionary decade — tens of thousands executed/died in the Terror, and, counting the Vendée and provincial repression, far more. Accurate as a general statement.

GC-15-134.rationale: Theological claim about Satan's policy and purposes. Supernatural; not testable.

GC-15-135.rationale: Theological claim about Satan re-masking error. Not testable.

GC-15-136.rationale: Interpretive/theological thesis (France's 'fatal error' was ignoring that freedom lies within God's law). Not empirically testable.

GC-15-137.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate KJV fragments: Isa 48:18, Isa 57:21, Prov 1:33.

GC-15-138.rationale: Factual core supported: the Convention (National Council) did set aside Christian law/worship in 1793. The 'cause and effect... seen by all' framing is White's providential-interpretive reading, hence low confidence on the whole.

GC-15-139.rationale: Interpretive/theological claim (all French institutions smitten for rejecting God's law). Providential.

GC-15-140.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate KJV fragments: Prov 11:5, Ecclesiastes 8:12-13, Prov 1:29,31.

GC-15-141.rationale: In 1793 the Convention enacted the dechristianization measures that suppressed Christian worship and set aside Scripture. Accurate.

sources.title: Dechristianization of France during the French Revolution

GC-15-142.rationale: The precise '3½ years later' interval mirrors the Rev 11 'three days and a half' and is prophetically driven. Historically, freedom of worship was substantially restored earlier — the law of 3 Ventôse Year III (21 Feb 1795), on the report of the Protestant Boissy d'Anglas, proclaimed separation of church and state and freedom of worship — roughly 15 months after the Nov 1793 measures, not 3½ years. Adventist historicists point instead to later 1797 acts; the exact dating is contested and interpretation-dependent.
GC-15-142.modern_consensus: Religious toleration was restored by stages, the key liberalization coming with the law of 21 Feb 1795.

sources.title: The restoration of religious freedom (law of 21 Feb 1795) — Musée protestant

GC-15-143.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate KJV renderings; note the second sentence is Jeremiah 16:21 (the claim_text correctly identifies it), the first Isaiah 37:23.

GC-15-144.rationale: Prophetic-interpretive claim (the Scriptures honored 'as never before' since 1793). Theological framing.

GC-15-145.rationale: The British and Foreign Bible Society was founded 7 March 1804, and similar societies with continental branches followed. Accurate.

GC-15-146.rationale: The American Bible Society was founded in May 1816 in New York. Accurate.

GC-15-147.rationale: The Bible had been printed in roughly 50 languages by the time of the BFBS's founding (1804), and by the early 20th c had been translated (in whole or part) into several hundred languages/dialects — 'more than four hundred' is consistent with the period figure.

sources.title: British and Foreign Bible Society — translations

GC-15-148.rationale: The modern Protestant foreign-missions movement is conventionally dated to the 1790s (William Carey's 1792 Enquiry and the Baptist Missionary Society); the preceding half-century saw comparatively little organized Protestant missionary activity. Accurate as a general statement.

GC-15-149.rationale: From the 1790s onward a wave of missionary societies formed (BMS 1792, LMS 1795, CMS 1799) and foreign missions grew rapidly; the framing links this to a reaction against rationalism, which is broadly consistent with the evangelical revival context.

sources.title: History of Protestant missions (late 18th c revival)

GC-15-150.rationale: [common_knowledge] 19th-c improvements in printing (steam presses, stereotyping, cheaper paper) greatly increased the volume and lowered the cost of Bible production. Textbook.

GC-15-151.rationale: By the late 19th/early 20th c the Bible was sold openly in Rome and circulated worldwide via the Bible societies. Consistent with the period.

GC-15-152.rationale: This 'boast' ('twelve men established... one man may suffice to overthrow it') is a stock apologetic attribution that cannot be located in Voltaire's authenticated works; it circulates almost exclusively in Christian polemical/apologetic literature and is regarded as apocryphal, part of a broader pattern of Voltaire misattributions (including the 'Bible Society in his house' legend). Attributed quote unlocatable in the primary corpus.
GC-15-152.modern_consensus: No sourcing to Voltaire's writings; treated as apocryphal.

sources.title: The Origins of a Warning from Voltaire — Cato Institute
Voltaire's prediction / Bible Society — truth or myth?
sources.note: position: even sympathetic writers cannot source the quote

GC-15-153.rationale: Voltaire died 30 May 1778; 'a century has passed since his death' fits the 1888 first edition (110 years) and the sentence's approximate framing. Accurate.

GC-15-154.rationale: A rhetorical statistic. The dramatic increase in Bible circulation between Voltaire's day and the late 19th c (via the Bible societies) is real; the specific hundred-to-ten-thousand/hundred-thousand ratios are illustrative rather than precise.

GC-15-155.rationale: The genuine proverb is 'the Church of God is an anvil that has worn out many hammers,' attributed to the Reformer Theodore Beza (reply to the King of Navarre, c.1562) and catalogued in standard proverb references as being about the Church. GC frames it as 'concerning the Christian church' yet renders the quoted words as 'The Bible is an anvil,' substituting 'the Bible' for 'the Church' — an altered quotation. The attribution to 'an early Reformer' is otherwise apt (Beza).
GC-15-155.modern_consensus: Standard form is 'the Church... is an anvil which has worn out many hammers' (Beza).

sources.title: Church is an anvil which has worn out many hammers — Oxford Reference
the Church is an anvil... — Dictionary of Proverbs

GC-15-157.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate KJV renderings of Isaiah 40:8 and Psalm 111:7-8.

GC-15-158.rationale: Theological/interpretive maxim (human authority overthrown, God's word stands). Not empirically testable.
